title: Orthogonal Polynomials and the MacWilliams Transform for Permutation-Invariant Qudit Codes
abstract: We derive an explicit formula for the intrinsic MacWilliams transform for permutation-invariant qudit codes. Such codes naturally live in symmetric power representations, where the relevant error sectors are determined by the irreducible decomposition of the conjugation action on the associated operator space. Using the multiplicity-free structure of this decomposition and the corresponding intertwiner algebra, we identify the intrinsic MacWilliams matrix with a finite Racah transform. The entries are given by a terminating hypergeometric series, and the rows of the matrix are Racah orthogonal polynomials with parameters determined explicitly by the block length and local dimension. Computing the spectrum of the degree-one twirl reveals that this spectrum lies on an affine quadratic lattice. Then we derive a tridiagonal multiplication rule from the representation theory of the adjoint sector. As consequences, we obtain closed-form orthogonality, detailed-balance, and involutivity identities for the transform. The resulting formula supplies an explicit MacWilliams matrix for computing linear programming bounds on permutation-invariant qudit codes.
summary: This theoretical paper derives the intrinsic MacWilliams transform for a specific class of quantum error-correcting codes (permutation-invariant qudit codes). It achieves this by mapping the problem onto the spectral theory of operators acting on symmetric tensor representations of SU(q). The main result is an explicit formula for the transform matrix, showing it is a finite Racah transform, which is crucial for establishing rigorous bounds on the code's performance.
main_problem: The paper aims to derive an explicit formula for the intrinsic MacWilliams transform associated with permutation-invariant qudit codes.
main_result: The intrinsic MacWilliams matrix is shown to be a finite Racah transform, providing an explicit, closed-form matrix for computing linear programming bounds on these codes.
method: The derivation heavily utilizes representation theory, relating the transform to the joint eigenvalues of twirling operators acting on symmetric power representations of SU(q).
model_or_system: The system studied is permutation-invariant qudit codes, whose underlying structure is analyzed using the symmetric power representation $V_n = 	ext{Sym}^n(\mathbb{C}^q)$ of the group SU(q).
key_observables: The intrinsic MacWilliams matrix $M = (M_{ba})$, the spectrum of the degree-one twirl operator $T_1$, and the associated Racah orthogonal polynomials.
important_parameters: The qudit dimension $q$, the block length $n$, and the indices $a, b$ corresponding to irreducible sectors.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the multiplicity-free decomposition of the operator space $\mathcal{L}(V_n)$ and the properties derived from Casimir operators in the su(q) algebra.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by establishing the spectral decomposition of the twirling operators, proving that the resulting polynomial representatives form an orthogonal system, identifying this system with Racah polynomials, and finally deriving the explicit matrix elements and proving structural identities like orthogonality and involutivity.
why_interesting: The deep connection between quantum error-correcting codes (qudit codes) and advanced mathematical structures like Racah polynomials and orthogonal polynomials provides powerful, explicit tools for bounding code performance.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
